title: Scope and closures

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific source-linked draft lesson for Scope and closures; independent technical, source, pedagogy, accessibility, and runnable-example review remains required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: The Python Language Reference; Python 3.14; Execution Model and Function Definitions: name binding, scopes, closures, calls, and defaults; atomic topic: Scope and closures
sources.claim: Python resolves names through local, enclosing, global, and built-in scopes; closures retain references to enclosing cells, with nonlocal or global required for rebinding those outer names.

sources.claim: Python resolves names through local, enclosing, global, and built-in scopes; closures retain references to enclosing cells, with nonlocal or global required for rebinding those outer names.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Python resolves names through local, enclosing, global, and built-in scopes; closures retain references to enclosing cells, with nonlocal or global required for rebinding those outer names.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A make_counter closure uses nonlocal count to rebind its enclosing integer and gives each factory call an independent cell. Your goal is to trace name lookup and implement a small closure without accidental shared mutation. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Core collections and text; pf abstraction calls; Parameter kinds and argument binding; pf abstraction scope. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language-specific operational model
sections.body: Python resolves names through local, enclosing, global, and built-in scopes; closures retain references to enclosing cells, with nonlocal or global required for rebinding those outer names. This model is applied through the declared Python: Beginner to Intermediate behavior rather than a language-neutral slogan.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure model
sections.body: Assignment makes a name local unless declared, loop-created closures can share one late-bound cell, mutable captured objects change without nonlocal, and global state couples tests. The learner must reject this documented misconception: A closure copies every captured value when it is created, so later changes to the enclosing binding cannot affect it.

includes: Resolve local, enclosing, global and built-in names and capture enclosing state deliberately.
Trace name lookup and implement a small closure without accidental shared mutation.
excludes: Assignment makes a name local unless declared, loop-created closures can share one late-bound cell, mutable captured objects change without nonlocal, and global state couples tests.

outcomes: Trace name lookup and implement a small closure without accidental shared mutation.

examples.title: Scope and closures: direct language case
examples.situation: A make_counter closure uses nonlocal count to rebind its enclosing integer and gives each factory call an independent cell.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the concrete Python: Beginner to Intermediate state or tool result: A make_counter closure uses nonlocal count to rebind its enclosing integer and gives each factory call an independent cell.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.

Step 3 — Verify the result with this correction: Closures retain enclosing bindings and usually use late lookup; bind a snapshot deliberately when needed and use nonlocal only for explicit enclosing rebinding.
examples.result: The example meets the atomic outcome because Closures retain enclosing bindings and usually use late lookup; bind a snapshot deliberately when needed and use nonlocal only for explicit enclosing rebinding.

examples.title: Scope and closures: boundary and transfer case
examples.situation: Callbacks created in a loop bind the current value through a default argument rather than all reading the final loop variable later.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name what changed from the direct case: Callbacks created in a loop bind the current value through a default argument rather than all reading the final loop variable later.

Step 2 — Keep the language boundary explicit: Assignment makes a name local unless declared, loop-created closures can share one late-bound cell, mutable captured objects change without nonlocal, and global state couples tests.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting but incorrect rule: A closure copies every captured value when it is created, so later changes to the enclosing binding cannot affect it.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Trace name lookup and implement a small closure without accidental shared mutation.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: A closure copies every captured value when it is created, so later changes to the enclosing binding cannot affect it.
misconceptions.correction: Closures retain enclosing bindings and usually use late lookup; bind a snapshot deliberately when needed and use nonlocal only for explicit enclosing rebinding.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Predict both examples and identify the first observable contradiction in this belief: A closure copies every captured value when it is created, so later changes to the enclosing binding cannot affect it.

analogy.example: a callable service counter with a published request form, binding rules, retained enclosing notes, and a recursive delegation limit can introduce Scope and closures, after the canonical Python: Beginner to Intermediate rule is stated first.
limitations: definition-time defaults, LEGB lookup, late binding, exceptions, and stack limits have no exact service-counter counterpart
